quick_index.title: Quick Index
quick_index.content:
ROUTER FIRST, then implementations.

- How To Use
- Always (every task)
- Router (task type to flow + headline reminder)
- Lifecycle (8 phases)
- Flows: direct answer, implementation (legacy / greenfield), bug, data query (diagnostic / prod), planning, testing, dev logging, review, message to a person, report + handoff, meta tooling
- Engineering capsules: coding, comments, testing + data safety, workflow + handoff

how_to_use.title: How To Use
how_to_use.content:
🧭 This is the main 'how to do things' router for business dev work.

For every task:
1. Load the communication subprofile first (default `foreign_legacy_code`) so tone, epistemic stance, and no-fabrication rules are active.
2. Match the request in `router` to the narrowest flow.
3. Open that flow: it lists what to REMEMBER and points to the engineering capsules for detail.
4. Apply `always` on top of every flow.

The router entry is short on purpose. The flow gives the reminders. The `eng_*` capsules hold the full detailed rules so flows do not duplicate them.

Company-agnostic and language-agnostic. No stack, framework, or vendor names live here.

always.title: 🔑 Always — every task
always.content:
Applies to every flow.

- 🗣️ Load the communication subprofile first (default `foreign_legacy_code`). It owns tone, the plain / technical split, and the writing mechanics.
- 📌 Read active project STATE when project state matters; skip it for a trivial question answerable from visible context.
- 🎯 Prefer the smallest read and the smallest ownership slice that answers or implements the request. Avoid broad sweeps when a narrow contract point solves it.
- 🧩 Separate observed facts, inference, speculation, and value judgment.
- 🚩 No fabrication: when a referenced doc, ticket field, sibling repo, or needed value is not visible, flag the gap plainly and immediately, do not invent it. Tag any unavoidable unknown grep-findably (TBC / OPEN / ASSUMED / MISSING-SOURCE). See subprofile section 'No fabrication'.
- 🧠 Unknown-unknown: when it is not known how a business rule, framework primitive, or integration is meant to work, say so. Do not extrapolate from naming, layout, imports, or comments as if they were a spec.
- 🛠️ Meta-tooling is a separate track (see `flow_meta_tooling`): never fold framework / pipeline / scratch / bootstrap work into a ticket's state, PLAN, PR, or comment.
- ✅ Preserve momentum when the request is clear. If new ambiguity appears mid-task that can change scope, behavior, priority, or acceptance, pause and ask.

router.title: 🧭 Router — if you do X, open flow Y
router.content:
Match the first row that fully covers the request. When a task spans rows, follow the strictest relevant approval / verification and run each sub-intent through its own flow.

| If the task is… | Flow | Headline reminder |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Answer a question / explain, no edits | `flow_direct_answer` | Smallest read; no edits; flag gaps instead of guessing |
| Write / change code in a foreign or legacy area | `flow_implementation_legacy` | Surgical + match surrounding conventions, but new code is modern and clean |
| Write code in a greenfield / owned area | `flow_implementation_greenfield` | Own the architecture: domain model, contracts, tests, future-proof when cheap |
| Investigate / fix a bug | `flow_bug` | Root cause at the owning boundary, not a symptom patch; add diagnostics before speculative fixes |
| Run a diagnostic data query | `flow_data_query_diagnostic` | Writable scratch schema, prod read-only, one batched result set where perf allows |
| Run a potentially expensive query on production | `flow_data_query_prod` | Gate with an instant metadata pre-check; abort with diagnostics if too costly; hard time ceiling |
| Plan a ticket / multi-step work | `flow_planning` | Read the WHOLE ticket incl. all comments + linked tickets; research-first; pick a shape |
| Test a change | `flow_testing` | Ask 'can we write here?' first; group cases by mutation risk; defense-in-depth = ship-readiness |
| Add logging to help the agent | `flow_dev_logging` | Gate by the canonical dev flag; gitignored scratch dir; fail silently |
| Review code / a PR / a plan | `flow_review` | Findings first by severity; reference as `File::method [line]`; do not edit unless asked |
| Ghost-write a chat message to a person | `flow_message_to_person` | Zero ceremony, lowercase, one thought per line, no tables |
| Report / prepare handoff | `flow_report_handoff` | Business storytelling; two-layer artifacts; user owns commit / PR / ticket lifecycle |
| Framework / pipeline / bootstrap work | `flow_meta_tooling` | Separate track; never in a ticket's state / PLAN / PR |

lifecycle.title: 🔁 Lifecycle — 8 phases for a ticketed task
lifecycle.content:
Linear with one branch: an easy single-file fix skips Plan.

1. 📚 Read — fetch the whole ticket + metadata, all comments, attachments, and every linked ticket (blocks / blocked-by / relates / parent / child). Flag inaccessible referenced docs; do not start designing on partial context.
2. ⚖️ Decide complexity — easy single-file fix with clear ownership: go straight to Implement. Multi-step / architectural / unclear scope: Plan first. When in doubt, Plan (plan cost is low, missed-plan cost is high).
3. 📐 Plan — research known approaches before custom design; pick a shape; save PLAN + update STATE before implementing. See `flow_planning`.
4. ✏️ Implement — execute per PLAN; the user reviews the code. See `flow_implementation_legacy` / `flow_implementation_greenfield`.
5. 🗣️ Report — business storytelling, no jargon. See `flow_report_handoff`.
6. 🧪 Test — auto where safe, manual scenarios where not. See `flow_testing`.
7. 🤝 Joint verification — user runs manual, agent runs automated, iterate until no signal fails. Do not close while anything is unverified.
8. 📤 Handoff — user does commit / push / PR / deployment plan / ticket close; agent prepares paste-ready draft text only. See `workflow_handoff`.

flow_direct_answer.title: 💬 Flow — Direct answer
flow_direct_answer.content:
When: a question, explanation, or factual project answer with no edits.

Remember:
- Read only the smallest source / doc / context slice needed to answer accurately.
- Do not edit files.
- State certainty; flag gaps and uncertainty rather than fabricate (see `always`).
- Deep, mechanistic conversational depth is allowed only in genuine chat / speculation, NEVER while executing a task. During task execution keep output lean (subprofile section 'Interaction defaults').

flow_implementation_legacy.title: 🧩 Flow — Implementation (foreign / legacy)
flow_implementation_legacy.content:
When: writing or changing code in a messy, undocumented, or inconsistent codebase the agent does not own.

Remember:
- Keep SCOPE surgical: a 3-line change is 3 lines. Match surrounding style and framework conventions. Do not migrate paradigms during a narrow ticket. Minimize blast radius.
- Keep QUALITY high in what you DO write: new code is modern and clean even inside old style (see `eng_coding`). Small scope and high quality are not in tension.
- Use helpers, adapters, seams, invariant checks, or diagnostics only when they reduce the immediate change risk. Do not force a clean-architecture (DDD/ADIV) rewrite into an old module.
- Search callers before editing or deleting (see `eng_coding` caller-search rules).
- Full rules: `eng_coding`, `eng_comments`.

flow_implementation_greenfield.title: 🌱 Flow — Implementation (greenfield / owned)
flow_implementation_greenfield.content:
When: writing code in a new or agent-owned area, or an actively modernized zone.

Remember:
- Own the architecture: clear domain model, explicit contracts and schemas, framework-native conventions.
- Use DDD-ADIV boundaries as the default shape when the change is large enough to warrant it.
- Add tests and diagnostics before complexity grows.
- Prefer a slightly better future-proof structure when the extra cost is small (better-version-now); run the overengineering check when the better version is large, speculative, or ceremony-heavy.
- Still research-first (borrowed-before-invented, web-research spike for non-obvious writes).
- Full rules: `eng_coding`, `eng_comments`.

flow_bug.title: 🐛 Flow — Bug
flow_bug.content:
When: investigating or fixing a defect, regression, or wrong behavior.

Remember:
- Find the source-owned ROOT CAUSE at the correct ownership boundary. Do not mask a symptom with a local patch or a swallowed error.
- When evidence is weak, add targeted diagnostics BEFORE repeated speculative fixes.
- Diagnosis ladder, cheapest first:
  1. Read the code path + existing logs.
  2. A read-only data query to confirm the actual state (see `flow_data_query_diagnostic`).
  3. If a query cannot reach the answer (the issue is in runtime flow, not stored data), add a dev-only instrumented entry point / probe route to observe the live path (gated dev-only, fail-silent — see `flow_dev_logging`).
- Once the root cause is proven, make the smallest owned fix.
- Report observed facts first, then likely cause with a confidence level (subprofile epistemic rules).

flow_data_query_diagnostic.title: 🔎 Flow — Data query (diagnostic)
flow_data_query_diagnostic.content:
When: querying a database to diagnose state, confirm a hypothesis, or gather evidence.

Remember:
- Run any writes or temporary objects in a WRITABLE SCRATCH schema. The production schema stays READ-ONLY. Never create objects in prod.
- Batch related probes into ONE result set (e.g. UNION ALL) where performance allows, so the reader gets a single grid, not N scattered outputs.
- Push an expensive unindexed-scan branch into a SEPARATE, explicitly-gated appendix, not the default grid.
- Report values in human-readable units.
- If the query hits production and could be expensive, use `flow_data_query_prod` instead.

flow_data_query_prod.title: 🚦 Flow — Data query (production cost guard)
flow_data_query_prod.content:
When: a potentially expensive query must run against production.

Remember:
- Gate it with an INSTANT metadata / catalog pre-check (row counts, index presence) wrapped so:
  - too expensive: return a diagnostics result set AND hard-abort, so the reason is visible from the output paste,
  - within budget: run the real query silently.
- Keep a HARD TIME CEILING (a few minutes max). Never let a diagnostic run unbounded.
- Combine with `flow_data_query_diagnostic` conventions (scratch schema for any writes, single batched grid).

flow_planning.title: 📐 Flow — Planning
flow_planning.content:
When: a multi-step, architectural, multi-file, or unclear-scope task needs a plan.

Remember:
- Read the WHOLE ticket first: description, acceptance criteria, ALL comments, attachments, metadata, AND every linked ticket (blocks / blocked-by / relates / parent / child). Do not plan on partial context.
- An inaccessible referenced doc: flag the gap, ask for it, do not fabricate its contents.
- Research-first: check known approaches and domain archetypes before custom design. Run a web-research spike for non-obvious writes (feature design, structural bugfix, performance, architecture) unless the plan already names the mechanism.
- Decide the implementation shape:
  - `surgical change` — minimal touch in the current file, match style,
  - `extract to helper / class` — the new code does not belong to the current file's business domain,
  - `clean domain module` — the change is large enough and the zone supports domain modeling,
  - `full rewrite of the touched module` — only when surgical / extract would create more debt than a rewrite.
  Drivers: change size, business-context fit, predictable load. Ask about lightweight-futureproof when future load is unclear.
- Ticket scope INCLUDES genuine workflow / dev-tooling / observability improvements found while doing the work. Default to bundling them into the same PR as a clearly separate section the reviewer can opt out of (project-side tooling only — framework / workspace edits are meta-tooling and do NOT ride along).
- Save PLAN and update STATE before implementation begins.

flow_testing.title: 🧪 Flow — Testing
flow_testing.content:
When: testing a change, or planning how it will be tested.

Remember:
- BEFORE any test that could mutate data (read OR write): determine and ASK the environment (prod / prod-clone / staging / throwaway) and 'CAN we do writes here?'. Default = NO until the user affirmatively confirms the env is safe.
- Group every proposed test case by mutation risk and tag it: [ZERO mutation by design] / [MUTATION - needs write approval] / [unknown - verify first].
- Defense-in-depth as ship-readiness: when full end-to-end UI smoke is impractical, do not block on it IF the stack has defense-in-depth (frontend guard + authoritative backend gate + data-layer validation). Write the worst case explicitly; if worst case is 'bad UX but safe data', ship and document the skip in the PR. Never accept this when the untested layer is the authoritative correctness gate.
- Write manual test scenarios for what cannot be safely auto-tested (UI clicks, OAuth, prod-only paths, third-party side effects): where to click / call, what input, success signal, failure signal, expected before / after state. The user runs it; the agent updates state with results.
- Full rules: `eng_testing`.

flow_dev_logging.title: 📝 Flow — Dev-only logging for the agent
flow_dev_logging.content:
When: adding logging / instrumentation to help the agent observe behavior during hybrid AI-tests or bug hunting.

Remember:
- Gate STRICTLY by the project's canonical dev-env flag. Never a framework default the team does not actually use.
- Write to the project's gitignored agent-scratch directory, not the project root. Keep it project-local, not shared across projects.
- Fail silently on write, so a failed log never breaks the request.
- Mind the process-user vs directory-owner permission mismatch; note the first-time setup step (e.g. making the scratch dir writable by the running process).
- This logging is dev-loop tooling: it may ride along in the ticket PR as a separate section (see `flow_planning`).

flow_review.title: 🔍 Flow — Review
flow_review.content:
When: reviewing code, a diff, a PR, or a plan.

Remember:
- Lead with FINDINGS, ordered by severity, before any summary.
- Reference locations as `File::method [line]` — name the file and the method / symbol. A bare line number is not human-cognitive; the method name anchors the reader.
- Prioritize bugs, regressions, unsafe assumptions, missing tests, and ownership violations.
- State open questions and assumptions AFTER the findings. Keep the summary brief and secondary.
- Do not edit files unless the user explicitly asks for fixes.

flow_message_to_person.title: ✉️ Flow — Message to a person
flow_message_to_person.content:
When: ghost-writing a chat message TO a colleague or stakeholder (Teams / Slack / email-chat).

Remember:
- Zero ceremony: no 'Hi X', no 'Thanks, Name' padding.
- Lowercase chat register; short; one thought per line.
- NO tables. Emoji OK. Plain human prose bullets.
- It must read like a human wrote it to a non-technical colleague.
- This is distinct from explaining TO the user (where tables + emoji are fine). Full rules: subprofile sections 'Interaction defaults' and 'Writing mechanics'.

flow_report_handoff.title: 📤 Flow — Report + handoff
flow_report_handoff.content:
When: reporting task results or preparing handoff artifacts.

Remember:
- Report in BUSINESS STORYTELLING: two compact sections in domain language, no jargon — 'what was needed / what the problem was' and 'what was done'. Technical detail stays in the diff and the technical sections, not in the storytelling.
- Put the storytelling in the chat reply AND in the working-memory handoff, so the user can paste it into a PR description, a ticket comment, or a deployment plan.
- Working-memory artifacts are two-layer: a 'simply put' plain-business layer ON TOP, technical detail below (subprofile 'Writing mechanics').
- Ownership boundary: the user owns commit, push, PR creation, ticket transitions, and the deployment plan. The agent prepares every paste-ready draft (PR body, ticket comment, deployment plan) but never INITIATES that lifecycle. Reading (PR view / diff, ticket read) is fine.
- Full rules: `workflow_handoff`.

flow_meta_tooling.title: 🛠️ Flow — Meta-tooling
flow_meta_tooling.content:
When: framework, pipeline, agent-scratch, bootstrap, profile, or workflow-config work.

Remember:
- This is how-we-work, separate from what-we-ship. It is NEVER a current ticket.
- Never fold it into the active ticket's state, PLAN, PR description, or ticket comment.
- Do not infer 'the pipeline is being changed because of ticket X'. The tracks are independent.
- Report meta-tooling changes in chat only, unless the user explicitly asks to log them somewhere durable.
- Conversely, ticket work never edits the shared framework or workspace-level pipeline files unless the user explicitly asks for a pipeline change.

eng_coding.title: 🧩 Capsule — Coding
eng_coding.content:
Two axes kept SEPARATE:
- SCOPE = surgical and minimal. A 3-line change is 3 lines. Do not push grand rewrites.
- QUALITY = clean in whatever is written or extracted: top-down (general to specific), interfaces / contracts on top with implementation hidden, divided into named responsibilities. Apply this EVEN when extracting into legacy directories.
Small scope and high quality are NOT in tension.

Style of NEW code:
- follow the user's stated guidelines OVER the style of the surrounding code (the codebase may be chaotic; do not mimic neighbouring ugly patterns).
- write NEW code in modern idioms even when surrounded by old style (modern naming, modern collection / array literals, proper imports over inline fully-qualified references used more than once).
- inconsistency between clean new code and ugly old code in the same file is acceptable; consistency-with-a-bad-pattern is not.
- language-level constants keep their universal convention (SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE).

Scope discipline:
- new code must work with the old, but DO NOT refactor neighbouring legacy.
- renaming an existing symbol used by many callers = touching surrounding code -> don't.
- modern idiom (full camelCase or the language's idiom) only for symbols YOU introduce.

DRY / extraction:
- if a validation / utility would be needed by 2+ callers (across classes OR within one class), EXTRACT it. Do not paste it twice 'for minimal blast radius'.
- as you write the SECOND copy, if the block is identical to the first -> STOP, extract.
- before writing a new helper, search for an existing one first.
- put shared pure utilities in the project's conventional shared-helper location; shared constants on a constants holder, not duplicated per class.

Defensive-coding balance:
- defending against malformed input in TWO layers can introduce a new failure mode (the defense itself crashes on an unexpected return shape / type).
- prefer ONE good check + a downstream business bound over double-defending.
- verify a language / runtime primitive's actual return contract before probing it; do not assume a shape that varies across versions.

Runtime-version safety:
- the host toolchain can lie about the runtime that actually executes the code.
- confirm the real runtime version (container / lockfile / manifest) and lint against IT, never against the host default. Avoid syntax the real runtime cannot parse.

Caller / usage search BEFORE editing or deleting:
- use TEXT search, UNRESTRICTED so ignored and hidden files are included (do not use a tracked-files-only search; do not rely on semantic 'find usages' alone — it is blind to dynamically-built calls).
- search BOTH call syntaxes: instance '->name(' and static '::name(' (a static method may also be called dynamically).
- in legacy code, if the whole-name search finds nothing, run a token-wildcard pass: split the name into tokens, search a case-insensitive regex with wildcards between the significant tokens, to catch names assembled from strings. Modern static-typed code does not need this.

eng_comments.title: 💬 Capsule — Comments
eng_comments.content:
- comment ONLY two cases, and only when non-obvious:
  1) 'we do it this way because X' — a hidden constraint, subtle invariant, surprising behavior, or a workaround for a specific bug,
  2) 'here we do Y' — a section marker for a non-obvious step in the flow.
- code explains itself: if a comment just restates what the code says, DELETE it.
- no ticket references in code (they live in history / PR / working memory).
- no TMI: no long rationale, no narration of old code, no 'why not the other thing', no future plans — that belongs in the PR / working memory / commit message.
- one thought per line; if a comment spans lines, each line is one short thought.
- docstrings: machine-readable param / return lines are fine; prose blocks stay minimal (the name + signature does the work).
- default: write ZERO comments; add one only when the WHY is genuinely non-obvious.
- test before adding: would removing this confuse a reader who knows nothing about the conversation that produced the code? If no -> don't write it.

eng_testing.title: 🧪 Capsule — Testing & data safety
eng_testing.content:
BEFORE any test that could mutate data — read OR write — determine and ASK:
- what environment does the test hit? (prod / prod-clone / staging / throwaway)
- CAN we do writes here? Default answer = NO until the user affirmatively confirms the env is safe for writes.

If writes are NOT allowed, the test plan is limited to:
- unit-level helper / pure-function tests (no controller, no DB),
- negative-path API tests where the guard denies BEFORE any save fires (mutation-safe by design — but verify with a before / after data snapshot),
- read-only path-order probes (nonexistent ids + invalid input — checks ordering without touching real rows),
- UI smoke where the frontend guard blocks before any backend call (zero data touch),
- code review for the positive path.

If writes ARE allowed, the plan may include full positive-path tests. Still note in the PR what mutated, so the reviewer knows.

Group every proposed test case by mutation risk and tag it explicitly:
  [ZERO mutation by design] / [MUTATION - needs write approval] / [unknown - verify first]
For path-order probes use nonexistent ids so that even if the guard breaks, the lookup fails first.

Defense-in-depth as ship-readiness:
- when full end-to-end UI smoke is impractical (scattered flows, no navigable path), do NOT block on it IF the stack has defense-in-depth: frontend guard + authoritative backend gate + data-layer validation. The backend layer is the floor.
- 'ship-ready' coverage then = helper unit tests (with boundaries + edges) + backend gate integration tests (each error convention) + before / after snapshot proving zero mutation + path-order probe + at least one partial UI smoke + code review for parallel paths.
- write the WORST-CASE explicitly in working memory: what happens if the specific untested layer fails. If worst case = 'bad UX but safe data' -> ship. Document the skip in the PR.
- do NOT apply this in greenfield with full test access — there, do everything.
- do NOT apply when the missing layer affects authoritative correctness (backend gate untested = unacceptable; frontend smoke untested = acceptable IF backend covered).

Manual test scenarios for what cannot be safely auto-tested (UI clicks, OAuth, prod-only paths, third-party side effects, write-and-rollback risk). In the PLAN write:
- where to click / what to call,
- what input to use,
- what signal = success, what signal = failure,
- expected before / after state.
The user runs it and reports back; the agent updates the state with results.

Dev-only logging added to help the agent (context harvest, observability during hybrid AI-tests):
- gate STRICTLY by the project's canonical dev-env flag (never a framework default that the team does not use),
- write to the project's gitignored agent-scratch directory, not the project root,
- keep it project-local, not shared across projects,
- fail silently on write so a failed log never breaks the request,
- mind the process-user vs dir-owner permission mismatch; note the first-time setup step.

workflow_handoff.title: 📤 Capsule — Workflow & handoff
workflow_handoff.content:
Task lifecycle (read -> decide complexity -> plan -> implement -> report -> test -> joint verification -> handoff):
- READ the whole ticket + linked tickets first; flag inaccessible referenced docs.
- DECIDE complexity: easy single-file fix -> do it; multi-step / architectural -> PLAN first.
- PLAN: research known approaches before custom design; decide shape (surgical / extract-to-helper / structured module / full rewrite) driven by change size, business fit, and predictable load. Save PLAN before implementing.
- IMPLEMENT per PLAN; the user reviews the code.
- REPORT in business storytelling (subprofile 'Writing mechanics').
- TEST per `eng_testing`.
- VERIFY jointly: user runs manual, agent runs automated, iterate.
- HANDOFF: draft every paste-able artifact (PR body, ticket comment, deployment plan) in working memory.

Ownership boundary (default in this work model):
- authored history and outward lifecycle stay with the USER: commit, push, PR creation, ticket transitions, wiki deployment plan.
- the agent prepares all draft text but never INITIATES the PR / ticket / wiki write lifecycle, unless the user explicitly delegates a specific one-off.
- reading (PR view / diff, ticket read) is fine.

Meta-tooling is NOT a ticket:
- framework / pipeline / agent-scratch / bootstrap work is how-we-work, separate from what-we-ship. Never fold it into the active ticket's state / PLAN / PR / comment.
- do not infer 'the pipeline is being changed because of ticket X'; the tracks are independent.

Workflow improvements ride along:
- genuine dev-loop / observability / refactor improvements found WHILE doing ticket work, that reduce future cost on similar work, DEFAULT to being included in the same PR.
- flag them as a separate PR section so the reviewer can opt out.
- the line: project-side dev tooling rides along; edits to the shared FRAMEWORK / workspace config do NOT (that is meta-tooling, above).

document.title: SocratexBusinessDev Workflow — how to do things

routing.purpose: Main 'how to do things' router for business dev work: classify the task, open the matching flow, apply the reminders. Router first, then flow implementations, then shared engineering capsules.
read_when: For every task under the SocratexBusinessDev work profile, as the operating router.
When PIPELINE-CONFIG.json selects SocratexBusinessDev as the work profile.
do_not_read_when: When a smaller current-state or subsystem capsule already answers the request.

metadata.extends: Communication style and codebase-reality stances (epistemic baseline, no-fabrication, interaction defaults, writing mechanics) live in the active communication subprofile (default core/communication-profiles/foreign_legacy_code.txt). This router owns the work rules; the subprofile owns tone and legacy-specific stances.
